title: Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A 5% growth rate is applied, modeling normalized cash flows over time as the utility executes its regulated capital investment plan and expands its rate base, generating predictable, moderate earnings growth.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A lower 7% discount rate reflects PEG's highly stable, regulated business model and low beta (0.58), signifying lower risk compared to the broader equity market.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2% terminal growth rate matches long-term inflation targets, appropriate for a utility whose growth is structurally tied to the baseline economic expansion of its local service territory.

thesisParagraphs: Public Service Enterprise Group operates as a highly dependable utility, providing essential electricity and gas services to a captive market in the Northeast. Its transition away from fossil fuels and strong reliance on heavily subsidized nuclear power make it a relatively clean energy player in the utility space. However, traditional DCF models often struggle with utilities due to their immense, ongoing capital expenditure requirements, which frequently result in minimal or even negative stated free cash flow.

Even when normalizing cash flows for this model, the current valuation indicates that the market is paying a significant premium for the perceived safety and yield of PEG shares. The stock appears overvalued relative to its raw cash-generating capabilities. Investors are likely valuing the company more on its dividend yield and regulated asset base than on standard free cash flow metrics.
verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: Why did Gemini pick a 5% growth rate for PEG?
faq.answer: The 5% growth rate mirrors the company's expected earnings growth driven by consistent, regulator-approved capital investments into modernizing the grid.

faq.question: What discount rate was used for PEG's DCF?
faq.answer: A relatively low 7% discount rate was chosen because utility companies, possessing regional monopolies, exhibit significantly lower systemic risk and volatility than typical corporations.

faq.question: Why is PEG rated OVERVALUED?
faq.answer: Utilities inherently require massive capital expenditures to maintain infrastructure, suppressing standard free cash flow. When valued strictly on a DCF basis, they often appear overvalued. The market frequently prices them as bond proxies based on yield rather than pure cash generation.
